perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party will: (a) limit disclosure of any Confidential Information to its #886871 2 directors, officers, employees, agents or representatives (collectively “ Representatives ”) who have a need to know such Confidential Information in connection with the current or contemplated business relationship between the parties to which this Agreement relates, and only for that purpose; (b) advise its Representatives of the proprietary nature of the Conf idential Information and of the obligations set forth in this Agreement , require such Representatives to be bound by written confidentiality restrictions no less stringent than those contained herein, and assume full liability for acts or omissions by its Representatives that are inconsistent with its obligations under this Agreement
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party will: (a) endeavor to limit disclosure of any Confidential Information to its directors, officers, employees, agents or representatives (collectively “ Representatives ”) who have a need to know such Confidential Information in connection with the current or contemplated business relationship between the parties to which this Agreement relates, and only for that purpose; (b) generally advise its Representatives of the proprietary nature of the Confidential Information and of the obligations set forth in this Agreement, and try to ensure Representatives are bound by written confidentiality restrictions , and assume liability for grossly negligent acts or omissions by its Representatives that are inconsistent with its obligations under this Agreement
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'limit disclosure' to 'endeavor to limit disclosure' weakens the obligation, making it less enforceable. Changing 'advise' to 'generally advise' reduces the rigor of informing representatives. Removing 'no less stringent than those contained herein' makes confidentiality restrictions potentially weaker, and changing 'full liability' to 'liability for grossly negligent acts or omissions' significantly limits the Receiving Party's responsibility for breaches by its representatives.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1714(a) states that everyone is responsible for an injury occasioned to another by his or her want of ordinary care or skill in the management of his or her property or person. Limiting the Receiving Party's liability to only 'grossly negligent acts' contradicts this general duty of care under California law, where the contract is to be performed.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1714 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 3. OBLIGATIONS IMPOSED BY LAW [1708 - 1725] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) 1714. (a) Everyone is responsible, not only for the result of his or her willful acts, but also for an injury occasioned to another by his or her want of ordinary care or skill in the management of his or her property or person, except so far as the latter has, willfully or by want of ordinary care, brought the injury upon himself or herself. The design, distribution, or marketing of firearms and ammunition is not exempt from the duty to use ordinary care and skill that is required by this section. The extent of liability in these cases is defined by the Title on Compensatory Relief. (b) It is the intent of the Legislature to abrogate the holdings in cases such as Vesely v. Sager (1971) 5 Cal.3d 153, Bernhard v. Harrah’s Club (1976) 16 Cal.3d 313, and Coulter v. Superior Court (1978) 21 Cal.3d 144 and to reinstate the prior judicial interpretation of this section as it relates to proximate cause for injuries incurred as a result of furnishing alcoholic beverages to an intoxicated person, namely that the furnishing of alcoholic beverages is not the proximate cause of injuries result
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts California Civil Code § 1714(a). The original text held the Receiving Party fully liable for acts or omissions by its Representatives. The changed text limits this liability to only 'grossly negligent acts or omissions.' This directly contradicts the legal standard of ordinary care outlined in the provided snippet from California Civil Code § 1714(a), which states, 'Everyone is responsible... for an injury occasioned to another by his or her want of ordinary care or skill.' By narrowing the liability to gross negligence, the changed text provides less protection than what is mandated by California law.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party shall immediately return and redeliver to Disclosing Party all tangible mater ial embodying any Confidential Information provided hereunder and all notes, summaries, memoranda, drawings, manuals, records, excerpts or derivative information deriving therefrom, and all other documents or materials (“Notes”) (and all copies of any of t he foregoing, including “copies” that have been converted to computerized media in the form of image, data , word processing , or other types of files either manually or by image capture) based on or including any Confidential Information, in whatever form o f storage or retrieval, upon the earlier of (i) the completion or termination of the dealings between the parties contemplated hereunder; (ii) the termination of this Agreement; or (iii) at such time as the Disclosing Party may so request
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party shall return and redeliver to Disclosing Party tangible material embodying Confidential Information and notes, summaries, memoranda, drawings, manuals, records, excerpts or derivative information deriving therefrom, and other documents or materials (“Notes”) based on or including Confidential Information, upon the earlier of (i) the completion or termination of the dealings between the parties contemplated hereunder; (ii) the termination of this Agreement; or (iii) at such time as the Disclosing Party may reasonably request
perturbation.explanation: Removing "immediately" from 'Receiving Party shall immediately return' introduces ambiguity regarding the timeframe for returning confidential information, potentially conflicting with trade secret laws which require prompt action to protect confidential information. Additionally, inserting "reasonably" before "request" gives the Receiving Party discretion to determine the reasonableness of the request, potentially delaying or preventing the return of confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) Cal. Civ. Code § 3426
perturbation.law_explanation: The CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. Delaying the return of confidential information or allowing discretion in determining the reasonableness of a return request undermines these reasonable efforts, potentially resulting in trade secret misappropriation if adequate measures are not taken promptly.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts trade secret law by removing 'immediately' and adding 'reasonably' before 'request', which introduces ambiguity and discretion in the return of confidential information. The Perturbation Explanation highlights this by stating that it potentially conflicts with trade secret laws requiring prompt action. The Law Explanation further supports this by stating that delaying the return or allowing discretion undermines reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy as required by CUTSA, potentially leading to misappropriation. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 only cites the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), it implies a requirement to protect trade secrets, which the modification weakens. The absence of a second snippet doesn't negate the contradiction, given the clear link between the altered text and the potential for delayed or prevented return of confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: The validity, construction and perfor mance of this Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of _____________________ (state) applicable to contracts made and to be wholly performed within such state, without giving effect to any conflict of laws provisions thereof . The Federal and state courts located in _______________ (state) shall have sole and exclusive jurisdiction over any disputes arising under , or in any way connected with or related to, the terms of this Agreement and Receiving Party: (i) consents to pe rsonal jurisdiction therein; and (ii) waives the right to raise forum non conveniens or any similar objection.
perturbation.changed_text: The validity, construction and performance of this Agreement shall be generally guided by the laws of _____________________ (state) applicable to contracts made and to be wholly performed within such state, with consideration given to any conflict of laws provisions thereof. The Federal and state courts located in _______________ (state) shall have jurisdiction over disputes arising under the terms of this agreement, and the Receiving Party: (i) consents to personal jurisdiction therein.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "shall be governed and construed in accordance with" to "shall be generally guided by" weakens the binding nature of the chosen state's laws. Adding "with consideration given to any conflict of laws provisions thereof" introduces uncertainty by potentially allowing other jurisdictions' laws to apply. Removing "sole and exclusive" from jurisdiction and removing the waiver of 'forum non conveniens' allow the Receiving Party to challenge the venue, potentially leading to litigation in a less convenient or favorable jurisdiction for the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.law_explanation: 28 U.S. Code § 1404(a) allows for a change of venue for the convenience of parties and witnesses, and in the interest of justice. While a forum selection clause (original text) can weigh heavily in the court's decision, it is not absolute. By removing the explicit waiver of 'forum non conveniens', the Receiving Party can argue for a transfer of venue under § 1404(a) even if the contract specifies a particular jurisdiction, undermining the Disclosing Party's reasonable expectation of litigating in the agreed-upon forum. Thus, the change creates an ambiguity that contradicts the intent of the original clause, making it more susceptible to challenge under 28 U.S. Code § 1404(a).

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) For the convenience of parties and witnesses, in the interest of justice, a district court may transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought or to any district or division to which all parties have consented. (b) Upon motion, consent or stipulation of all parties, any action, suit or proceeding of a civil nature or any motion or hearing thereof, may be transferred, in the discretion of the court, from the division in which pending to any other division in the same district . Transfer of proceedings in rem brought by or on behalf of the United States may be transferred under this section without the consent of the United States where all other parties request transfer. (c) A district court may order any civil action to be tried at any place within the division in which it is pending. (d) Transfers from a district court of the United States to the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, or the District Court of the Virgin Islands shall not be permitted under this section. As otherwise used in this section, the term “ district court ” includes the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, and the District Court of the Virgin Islands, and the term “district” includes the territorial jurisdiction of each such court. (June 25, 1948, ch. 646, 62 Stat. 937 ; Pub. L. 87–845, § 9 , Oct. 18, 1962 , 76A Stat. 699 ; Pub. L. 104–317, title VI, § 610(a) , Oct. 19, 1996 , 110 Stat. 3860 ; Pub. L. 112–63, title II, § 204 , Dec. 7, 2011 , 125 Stat. 764 .) Historical and Revision Notes Based on title 28, U.S.C., 1940 ed., §§ 119 , 163 (Mar. 3, 1911, ch. 231, § 58, 36 Stat. 1103 ; Sept. 8, 1916, ch. 475, § 5, 39 Stat. 851 ). Section co
perturbation.justification: The original text's forum selection clause provided a clear and agreed-upon venue for disputes. The changed text weakens this by removing 'sole and exclusive' jurisdiction and waiving 'forum non conveniens', which allows the Receiving Party to argue for a different venue based on convenience. 28 U.S. Code § 1404(a), as cited in both scraped snippets, states that "For the convenience of parties and witnesses, in the interest of justice, a district court may transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought or to any district or division to which all parties have consented." By removing the waiver, the Receiving Party can invoke this law to potentially move the case, directly contradicting the original intent of the forum selection clause and thus creating an ambiguity that undermines its enforceability.
